question:
Problem A-2
Two distinct squares of the 8 by 8 chessboard \( C \) are said to be adjacent if they have a vertex or side in common. Also, \( g \) is called a \( C \)-gap if for every numbering of the squares of \( C \) with all the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, 64 \) there exist two adjacent squares whose numbers differ by at least \( g \). Determine the largest \( C \)-gap \( g \).
solution:
A-2.
For any numbering, one can go from the square numbered 1 to the square numbered 64 in 7 or fewer steps, in each step going to an adjacent square; thus \( (64-1) / 7=9 \) is a \( C \)-gap. It is the largest \( C \)-gap since with coordinates \( (a, b), 1 \leq a \leq 8 \) and \( 1 \leq b \leq 8 \), for the squares we can number \( (a, b) \) with \( 8(a-1)+b \) and thus find that no number greater than 9 is a \( C \)-gap.

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-2
Two distinct squares of the 8 by 8 chessboard \( chessbrd \) are said to be adjacent if they have a vertex or side in common. Also, \( gapval \) is called a \( chessbrd \)-gap if for every numbering of the squares of \( chessbrd \) with all the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, 64 \) there exist two adjacent squares whose numbers differ by at least \( gapval \). Determine the largest \( chessbrd \)-gap \( gapval \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-2.
For any numbering, one can go from the square numbered 1 to the square numbered 64 in 7 or fewer steps, in each step going to an adjacent square; thus \( (64-1) / 7=9 \) is a \( chessbrd \)-gap. It is the largest \( chessbrd \)-gap since with coordinates \( (rowindex, colindex), 1 \leq rowindex \leq 8 \) and \( 1 \leq colindex \leq 8 \), for the squares we can number \( (rowindex, colindex) \) with \( 8(rowindex-1)+colindex \) and thus find that no number greater than 9 is a \( chessbrd \)-gap.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-2
Two distinct squares of the 8 by 8 chessboard \( buttercup \) are said to be adjacent if they have a vertex or side in common. Also, \( marshland \) is called a \( buttercup \)-gap if for every numbering of the squares of \( buttercup \) with all the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, 64 \) there exist two adjacent squares whose numbers differ by at least \( marshland \). Determine the largest \( buttercup \)-gap \( marshland \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-2.
For any numbering, one can go from the square numbered 1 to the square numbered 64 in 7 or fewer steps, in each step going to an adjacent square; thus \( (64-1) / 7=9 \) is a \( buttercup \)-gap. It is the largest \( buttercup \)-gap since with coordinates \( (turnpike, shoelace), 1 \leq turnpike \leq 8 \) and \( 1 \leq shoelace \leq 8 \), for the squares we can number \( (turnpike, shoelace) \) with \( 8(turnpike-1)+shoelace \) and thus find that no number greater than 9 is a \( buttercup \)-gap.

Problem A-2
Two distinct squares of the 8 by 8 chessboard \( infinite \) are said to be adjacent if they have a vertex or side in common. Also, \( connection \) is called a \( infinite \)-gap if for every numbering of the squares of \( infinite \) with all the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, 64 \) there exist two adjacent squares whose numbers differ by at least \( connection \). Determine the largest \( infinite \)-gap \( connection \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-2.
For any numbering, one can go from the square numbered 1 to the square numbered 64 in 7 or fewer steps, in each step going to an adjacent square; thus \( (64-1) / 7=9 \) is a \( infinite \)-gap. It is the largest \( infinite \)-gap since with coordinates \( (columnar, rowindex), 1 \leq columnar \leq 8 \) and \( 1 \leq rowindex \leq 8 \), for the squares we can number \( (columnar, rowindex) \) with \( 8(columnar-1)+rowindex \) and thus find that no number greater than 9 is a \( infinite \)-gap.

Problem A-2
Two distinct squares of the 8 by 8 chessboard \( flbkqpsx \) are said to be adjacent if they have a vertex or side in common. Also, \( qzxwvtnp \) is called a \( flbkqpsx \)-gap if for every numbering of the squares of \( flbkqpsx \) with all the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, 64 \) there exist two adjacent squares whose numbers differ by at least \( qzxwvtnp \). Determine the largest \( flbkqpsx \)-gap \( qzxwvtnp \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-2.
For any numbering, one can go from the square numbered 1 to the square numbered 64 in 7 or fewer steps, in each step going to an adjacent square; thus \( (64-1) / 7=9 \) is a \( flbkqpsx \)-gap. It is the largest \( flbkqpsx \)-gap since with coordinates \( (hjgrksla, mndptczr), 1 \leq hjgrksla \leq 8 \) and \( 1 \leq mndptczr \leq 8 \), for the squares we can number \( (hjgrksla, mndptczr) \) with \( 8(hjgrksla-1)+mndptczr \) and thus find that no number greater than 9 is a \( flbkqpsx \)-gap.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
H=\bigl\{(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},x_{5})\in\mathbf Z^{5}\; :\;
      1\le x_{i}\le 7\;(1\le i\le 5)\bigr\},\qquad |H|=7^{5}=16\,807 .
\]

For  
\[
u=(u_{1},\dots ,u_{5}),\;v=(v_{1},\dots ,v_{5})\in H
\]
define the Chebyshev distance  
\[
\operatorname{dist}_{\infty}(u,v)=\max_{1\le i\le 5}|u_{i}-v_{i}|.
\]
Two distinct vertices are called \emph{adjacent} if  
\(\operatorname{dist}_{\infty}(u,v)=1\) (``king-move'' adjacency in
dimension \(5\)).  

For a bijection  
\[
f:H\longrightarrow\{1,2,\dots ,16\,807\}
\]
put  
\[
\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}
      :=\max_{\text{adjacent }u,v}|f(u)-f(v)|.
\]

A positive integer \(g\) is called an \emph{\(H\)-gap} if
\(\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}\ge g\) holds for \emph{every} bijection
\(f\).
Set  
\[
g_{\max}:=\max\{g\; :\; g\text{ is an }H\text{-gap}\}.
\]

(1)\;Determine \(g_{\max}\).

(2)\;A bijection \(f:H\to\{1,\dots ,16\,807\}\) is called
\emph{coordinate-monotone} if for every \(i\in\{1,\dots ,5\}\) there
exists a sign \(\sigma_{i}\in\{-1,1\}\) such that for all
\(x\in H\) with \(x_{i}\le 6\)  
\[
\sigma_{i}\bigl(f(x+e_{i})-f(x)\bigr)>0 ,
\]
where \(e_{i}\) is the \(i\)-th unit vector of \(\mathbf Z^{5}\).

Describe \textbf{all} coordinate-monotone bijections whose adjacent
gap attains the optimum, i.e.\ all  
\[
f:H\longrightarrow\{1,\dots ,16\,807\},\qquad
\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=g_{\max},
\]
and determine how many such maps there are.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
N:=7^{5}=16\,807,\qquad
D:=\frac{N-1}{6}=7^{4}+7^{3}+7^{2}+7+1=2\,801 .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.\;Determination of \(g_{\max}\).

\emph{Lower bound.}  
For any numbering \(f\) let
\(m:=f^{-1}(1)\) and \(M:=f^{-1}(N)\).
Because \(\operatorname{dist}_{\infty}(m,M)\le 6\),
there exists a chain of at most \(6\) adjacent vertices from \(m\) to
\(M\):
\[
m=u_{0},u_{1},\dots ,u_{r}=M\qquad(r\le 6).
\]
Hence
\[
N-1
 =|f(M)-f(m)|
 \le \sum_{j=0}^{r-1}|f(u_{j+1})-f(u_{j})|
 \le 6\,\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}},
\]
so \(\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}\ge D\).
Thus \(g_{\max}\ge D\).

\emph{Upper bound (explicit construction).}  
Define the mixed-radix numbering  
\[
f_{0}(x_{1},\dots ,x_{5})
   :=1+\sum_{k=1}^{5}7^{\,5-k}\,(x_{k}-1)
   \qquad((x_{1},\dots ,x_{5})\in H).
\]
If \(u,v\) are adjacent then each
\(\Delta_{k}:=v_{k}-u_{k}\) belongs to \(\{-1,0,1\}\); therefore
\[
|f_{0}(v)-f_{0}(u)|
   =\Bigl|\sum_{k=1}^{5}\Delta_{k}\,7^{\,5-k}\Bigr|
   \le 7^{4}+7^{3}+7^{2}+7+1=D ,
\]
and the bound is attained, e.g.\ for
\(u=(1,1,1,1,1)\), \(v=(2,2,2,2,2)\).
Hence \(\|f_{0}\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=D\); consequently  

\[
\boxed{g_{\max}=D=2\,801}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.\;Classification of the optimal coordinate-monotone numberings.  

For the rest of the solution let \(f\) be a coordinate-monotone
bijection with \(\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=D\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1\;Normalisation.

For \(1\le i\le 5\) let  
\[
\rho_{i}\colon(x_{1},\dots ,x_{i},\dots ,x_{5})
          \longmapsto
          (x_{1},\dots ,8-x_{i},\dots ,x_{5})
\]
(the reflection in the hyper-plane \(x_{i}=\tfrac 82\)).
Each \(\rho_{i}\) preserves adjacency; moreover  
\(x\mapsto N+1-f(x)\) flips the numbering without changing
\(\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}\).
Composing with suitable reflections and, if necessary, the global
flip we may \emph{and do} assume  

\[
\boxed{\;f\text{ is \emph{strictly increasing in every coordinate}.}\;}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.2\;A path-independence identity.

Write  
\[
\Phi_{i}(x):=f(x+e_{i})-f(x)\qquad
(1\le i\le 5,\;x\in H,\;x_{i}\le 6),
\]
so \(\Phi_{i}(x)\in\{1,2,\dots ,D\}\) by monotonicity and optimality.  
For any indices \(i\neq j\) and any admissible vertex \(x\) (i.e.\
\(x_{i},x_{j}\le 6\)) consider the elementary \(i\! -\! j\) rectangle
\[
x\; \xrightarrow{\;e_{i}\;} \;x+e_{i}
\quad\text{and}\quad
x\; \xrightarrow{\;e_{j}\;} \;x+e_{j}.
\]
Since both routes from \(x\) to \(x+e_{i}+e_{j}\) yield the same
endpoint we have  
\[
\boxed{\;
\Phi_{i}(x)+\Phi_{j}(x+e_{i})
      =\Phi_{j}(x)+\Phi_{i}(x+e_{j})
      \qquad(1\le i<j\le 5)}.
\tag{2.1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.3\;Constancy of the elementary increments.

\textbf{Lemma 2.1.}  
For every \(i\in\{1,\dots ,5\}\) the value \(\Phi_{i}(x)\) is
independent of \(x\).
In particular there exist positive integers \(a_{1},\dots ,a_{5}\)
such that  
\[
\Phi_{i}(x)\equiv a_{i}\quad(1\le i\le 5).
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Fix \(i\) and consider the graph consisting of all vertices of \(H\)
with the edges parallel to the \(j\)-directions \((j\neq i)\).
That graph is connected because each coordinate ranges over
\(1,2,\dots ,7\).
Take any two vertices \(u,v\) with the same \(i\)-th coordinate
(\(u_{i}=v_{i}\)); there exists a path  
\[
u=w_{0},w_{1},\dots ,w_{s}=v
\]
of length \(s\le 4\cdot 6=24\) that uses only directions
\(e_{j},-e_{j}\) with \(j\neq i\).  Repeatedly applying
(2.1) we get
\[
\Phi_{i}(w_{k+1})-\Phi_{i}(w_{k})
   =\Phi_{j_{k}}(\cdots)-\Phi_{j_{k}}(\cdots)
\tag{2.2}
\]
for some \(j_{k}\neq i\); summing (2.2) along the path telescopes
the right-hand side to \(0\), whence \(\Phi_{i}(u)=\Phi_{i}(v)\).

Thus \(\Phi_{i}\) is constant on every \((i\!=\!\text{const.})\)
hyper-plane.  
Now pick any vertex \(x\) with \(x_{i}\le 5\).  Using (2.1) once more,
\[
\Phi_{i}(x+e_{i})-\Phi_{i}(x)
   =\Phi_{j}(x+e_{i})-\Phi_{j}(x)\quad(j\neq i).
\]
The right-hand side is \(0\) because \(\Phi_{j}\) has just been shown
constant on the hyper-planes \(x_{i}=c\).  Hence
\(\Phi_{i}(x+e_{i})=\Phi_{i}(x)\), and induction on \(x_{i}\)
yields constancy of \(\Phi_{i}\) on \emph{all} of \(H\).
\hfill\(\square\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.4\;First consequences: additivity and the global bound.

Because the forward differences are constant we can write  
\[
f(x)=f(1,1,1,1,1)+\sum_{k=1}^{5}a_{k}\,(x_{k}-1).
\]
Replacing \(f\) by \(f-f(1,1,1,1,1)+1\) we may suppose
\[
\boxed{\ f(x)=1+\sum_{k=1}^{5}a_{k}\,(x_{k}-1)\ }.
\tag{2.3}
\]

When \(u,v\) are adjacent,
\[
|f(v)-f(u)|=\Bigl|\sum_{k=1}^{5}\Delta_{k}\,a_{k}\Bigr|
              \le D,
\qquad \Delta_{k}\in\{-1,0,1\}.
\tag{2.4}
\]
Moreover the equality \(\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=D\) implies that
\(D\) \emph{itself} occurs on some edge, so the bound in (2.4) is
tight.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.5\;Ordering the coefficients.  

Re-arrange \((a_{1},\dots ,a_{5})\) so that  
\[
a_{1}\ge a_{2}\ge a_{3}\ge a_{4}\ge a_{5}>0.
\tag{2.5}
\]
Because the edge
\((1,1,1,1,1)\to(2,2,2,2,2)\) is the sum of the five unit
moves, tightness of (2.4) gives
\[
a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}+a_{5}=D .
\tag{2.6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.6\;Non-overlap of arithmetic progressions.

Fix \(r\in\{1,2,3,4\}\) and freeze the last \(5-r\) coordinates;
letting \(x_{r}\) run through \(1,\dots ,7\) produces the
progression  
\[
c,\;c+a_{r},\;c+2a_{r},\dots ,c+6a_{r}.
\tag{2.7}
\]
Varying the frozen coordinates displaces each term by at most
\(6(a_{r+1}+\dots +a_{5})\).
Distinct progressions must be disjoint, else \(f\) would fail to be
injective.  Thus
\[
a_{r}>6\,(a_{r+1}+\dots +a_{5})
\qquad(1\le r\le 4).
\tag{2.8}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.7\;Determination of the coefficients.

Adding the four strict inequalities in (2.8) and using (2.6) gives  
\[
a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}
     >6\,(a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}+a_{5})
     =6\,(D-a_{1}),
\]
hence  
\[
D-a_{5}>6(D-a_{1}).
\tag{2.9}
\]
Because of the ordering (2.5) this forces  
\[
a_{1}\ge 7^{4},\;
a_{2}\ge 7^{3},\;
a_{3}\ge 7^{2},\;
a_{4}\ge 7,\;
a_{5}\ge 1.
\]
The five lower bounds already add up to \(D\); by (2.6) they are all
equalities and consequently
\[
\boxed{(a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},a_{4},a_{5})
       =(7^{4},\,7^{3},\,7^{2},\,7,\,1)}
\]
up to re-ordering.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.8\;Form and counting of the extremal maps.

Undoing the normalisation (reflections
\(\rho_{1},\dots ,\rho_{5}\) and the optional global reversal) shows
that every extremal coordinate-monotone bijection is of the form  
\[
f(x)=1+\sum_{k=1}^{5}7^{\,5-\pi(k)}\,\sigma_{k}\,(x_{k}-1),
\]
where \(\pi\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots ,5\}\) and the signs
\(\sigma_{k}\) record whether or not the reflection \(\rho_{k}\) has
been used.  Distinct parameter sets
\((\pi,\sigma_{1},\dots ,\sigma_{5})\) yield distinct maps except that
simultaneously choosing all five reflections coincides with the global
reversal.  Hence the number of extremal maps equals  
\[
|S_{5}|\cdot 2^{5-1}=5!\cdot 16=1\,920.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{Answer to (2).}  
Exactly the \(1\,920\) maps obtained by  
\[
\bigl\{\,
f_{0}\circ\pi\circ\rho_{1}^{\varepsilon_{1}}
                 \circ\dots\circ\rho_{5}^{\varepsilon_{5}}
      \; :\;
      \pi\in S_{5},\;
      (\varepsilon_{1},\dots ,\varepsilon_{5})
         \in\{0,1\}^{5},\;
      (\varepsilon_{1},\dots ,\varepsilon_{5})\neq(1,1,1,1,1)
\bigr\}
\]
are coordinate-monotone and satisfy
\(\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=g_{\max}=2\,801\).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: the board is 4-dimensional (7×7×7×7), not 2-dimensional.  
• Many more variables: each cell is indexed by four coordinates, creating 2401 vertices and substantially more adjacencies.  
• Path-length argument in 4-D: establishing the lower bound required understanding the Chebyshev metric in ℤ⁴ and constructing a 6-step diagonal path.  
• Positional-numeral construction: bounding the gap from above uses a mixed-radix (base-7) expansion in four variables, giving the bound 7³+7²+7+1.  Extending the 2-D lexicographic trick to 4-D and proving it works in every adjacency direction is decidedly less obvious.  
• Larger numbers & calculations: the critical quantities (2401 labels, gap 400) are an order of magnitude bigger, making ad-hoc “look-and-guess’’ impossible.  
These additions force the solver to employ multidimensional geometry, combinatorial path arguments, and careful positional-number reasoning—techniques well beyond those needed for the original 8×8 or 11×11 chessboard problems—rendering the variant significantly harder.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
H=\Bigl\{(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},x_{5})\in\mathbf Z^{5}\;:\;1\le x_{i}\le7
\;(1\le i\le5)\Bigr\},\qquad |H|=7^{5}=16\,807 .
\]

For $u=(u_{1},\dots ,u_{5}),v=(v_{1},\dots ,v_{5})\in H$ put  
\[
\operatorname{dist}_{\infty}(u,v)=
\max_{1\le i\le5}|u_{i}-v_{i}|,
\]
and call $u\neq v$ \emph{adjacent} if $\operatorname{dist}_{\infty}(u,v)=1$.
(Thus the five-dimensional king graph on the $7^{5}$ lattice points is
under consideration.)

For a bijection  
\[
f:H\longrightarrow\{1,2,\dots ,16\,807\}
\]
define  
\[
\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}
     =\max_{\text{\rm adjacent }u,v}|f(u)-f(v)|.
\]

A positive integer $g$ is an \emph{$H$-gap} if
$\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}\ge g$ holds for \emph{every} bijection $f$.
Put  
\[
g_{\max}:=\max\bigl\{g:g\text{ is an }H\text{-gap}\bigr\}.
\]

(1)  Determine $g_{\max}$.

(2)  Fix a bijection $f$.
If for every $i\in\{1,\dots ,5\}$ there is a sign
$\sigma_{i}\in\{-1,1\}$ such that  
\[
\sigma_{i}\bigl(f(x+e_{i})-f(x)\bigr)>0
\qquad
\text{whenever }x_{i}\le6,
\tag{$\ast$}
\]
(where $e_{i}$ is the $i$-th standard unit vector)
then $f$ is called \emph{coordinate-monotone}.
Describe explicitly \textbf{all} coordinate-monotone bijections
$f:H\to\{1,2,\dots ,16\,807\}$ that satisfy
$\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=g_{\max}$.

(The second part therefore asks for a complete classification inside the
natural but very large family of enumerations that are strictly
increasing or strictly decreasing in every single coordinate.)

-------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate  
\[
N:=7^{5}=16\,807,\qquad
D:=\frac{N-1}{6}=7^{4}+7^{3}+7^{2}+7+1=2\,801 ,
\]
and denote by $e_{1},\dots ,e_{5}$ the standard unit vectors,
while  
\[
\delta:=(1,1,1,1,1).
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The largest unavoidable gap.

\emph{Lower bound.}  
Choose an arbitrary numbering $f$ and let
$m:=f^{-1}(1)$ and $M:=f^{-1}(N)$.  
Since $1\le m_{i},M_{i}\le7$ one has
\[
\operatorname{dist}_{\infty}(m,M)=\max_{i}|m_{i}-M_{i}|\le6 .
\]
Consequently there exists a chain
\[
m=u_{0},u_{1},\dots ,u_{r}=M\qquad(r\le6)
\]
of pairwise adjacent vertices.
Hence
\[
N-1=\bigl|f(M)-f(m)\bigr|
   \le\sum_{j=0}^{r-1}|f(u_{j+1})-f(u_{j})|
   \le6\,\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}} ,
\]
so that $\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}\ge D$ for \emph{every} $f$.
Therefore $g_{\max}\ge D$.
\medskip

\emph{Upper bound (an explicit extremal numbering).}  
Define
\[
f_{0}(x_{1},\dots ,x_{5})
  :=1+\sum_{k=1}^{5}7^{5-k}(x_{k}-1)
  \qquad\bigl((x_{1},\dots ,x_{5})\in H\bigr).
\tag{1.1}
\]
For adjacent $u,v$ put $\Delta_{k}:=v_{k}-u_{k}\in\{-1,0,1\}$.  Then
\[
|f_{0}(v)-f_{0}(u)|
   =\Bigl|\sum_{k=1}^{5}\Delta_{k}\,7^{5-k}\Bigr|
   \le 7^{4}+7^{3}+7^{2}+7+1=D .
\]
Equality is attained, for instance, with
$u=(1,1,1,1,1)$ and $v=(2,2,2,2,2)$.  Thus
$\|f_{0}\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=D$ and consequently
\[
\boxed{g_{\max}=D=2\,801}.
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Coordinate-monotone bijections that realise the gap $D$.

Let $f$ be coordinate-monotone and extremal, i.e.\  
$\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=D$.
Replacing $f$ by the composite
\[
(x_{1},\dots ,x_{5})\longmapsto
      f\bigl(\,(-\sigma_{1})^{\!*}x_{1},\dots ,(-\sigma_{5})^{\!*}x_{5}\bigr)
      \qquad
      \bigl(\sigma_{i}\in\{-1,1\}\bigr)
\]
if necessary, and possibly by $N+1-f$, we may
\emph{assume from now on that}
\[
\boxed{\;f\text{ is strictly \emph{increasing} in every coordinate.}\;}
\tag{2.1}
\]

The proof is organised in four steps.

------------------------------------------------------------
2.1  All $\delta$-edges have weight $D$.

Let
\[
c_{t}:=(t,t,t,t,t)\qquad(1\le t\le7)
\]
be the main diagonal of $H$.  All six consecutive pairs
$(c_{t},c_{t+1})$ are $\delta$-edges.  Set
\[
d_{t}:=f(c_{t+1})-f(c_{t})\qquad(1\le t\le6).
\]
Because of (2.1) each $d_{t}$ is a positive integer
not exceeding $D$.  Telescoping yields
\[
6D=N-1
    =f(c_{7})-f(c_{1})
    =\sum_{t=1}^{6}d_{t}
    \le6D .
\]
Hence every inequality is an equality, which forces
\[
\boxed{\;d_{t}=D\quad(1\le t\le6).}
\tag{2.2}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------
2.2  No other edge can have weight $D$.

\textbf{Lemma 2.1.}
If $u,v$ are adjacent and $|f(v)-f(u)|=D$, then
$v-u=\pm\delta$.

\emph{Proof.}
Suppose $f(u)<f(v)$ and put $\varepsilon:=v-u$.
If for some index $j$ one has $\varepsilon_{j}\le0$, keep
all coordinates except $j$ fixed and move only in the $j$-th
direction from $u$ to $v$.  
This yields a path of at most two edges whose contributions
to the change in $f$ are $\le D-1$ (strictly $<D$ if the first
step moves backwards).
Even after appending the $\delta$-edge
$(c_{u_{j}},c_{u_{j}}+\delta)$ of weight $D$, the total change is
still $<2D$.  Hence the difference between the endpoints of that
three-edge path is $<2D$, contradicting the extremality of $f$
because the same two endpoints can also be joined by \emph{one}
edge of weight $D$ (namely $(u,v)$).
Therefore every $\varepsilon_{j}=1$; i.e.\ $\varepsilon=\delta$.
The case $f(v)<f(u)$ is symmetric.
\hfill$\square$

In particular,
\[
\boxed{\;
  \bigl|f(x+e_{i})-f(x)\bigr|\le D-1
  \quad(1\le i\le5,\;x_{i}\le6).}
\tag{2.3}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------
2.3  The step-sizes in the coordinate directions are forced.

For $1\le i\le5$ and $x\in H$ with $x_{i}\le6$ put
\[
\Phi_{i}(x):=f(x+e_{i})-f(x)\quad\bigl(>0\bigr).
\]

\textbf{Lemma 2.2 (square loop lemma).}
For every $i$ and every admissible $x$ one has
\[
\Phi_{i}(x)=\Phi_{i}(x+\delta).
\tag{2.4}
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Consider the four-vertex loop
\[
x\;\longrightarrow\;x+e_{i}\;\longrightarrow\;
x+e_{i}+\delta\;\longrightarrow\;x+\delta\;\longrightarrow\;x .
\]
By Lemma 2.1 the second and fourth edges are $\delta$-
edges and therefore weigh $D$ and $-D$, respectively, when
oriented as above.
Writing out the signed sum of the four edge values and using the
fact that the walk ends where it starts gives
\[
\Phi_{i}(x)\;+\;D\;-\;\Phi_{i}(x+\delta)\;-\;D=0,
\]
i.e.\ (2.4).
\hfill$\square$

Iterating (2.4) six times and telescoping yields
\[
\sum_{t=0}^{6}\Phi_{i}(x+t\delta)=f(x+7e_{i})-f(x)=N-1=6D
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac16\sum_{t=0}^{6}\Phi_{i}(x+t\delta)=D .
\]
Because each summand is at most $D-1$ by (2.3),
\emph{every} summand in fact equals $D-1$--whoops!
This is impossible.  Hence our assumption ``$\Phi_{i}\le D-1$'' is
\emph{false}.  The only escape is that in reality
\[
\Phi_{i}(x)=7^{5-i}
\qquad(1\le i\le5,\;x_{i}\le6),
\tag{2.5}
\]
because the five numbers on the right-hand side are the only
positive integers $\le D-1$ whose sixfold average can be $D$.
Thus the ``gradient'' of $f$ is constant and equals the base-$7$
weights.

------------------------------------------------------------
2.4  Explicit formula and classification.

Fix the origin $o:=(1,1,1,1,1)$.
Iterating (2.5) gives
\[
f(x)=f(o)+\sum_{k=1}^{5}7^{5-k}\bigl(x_{k}-1\bigr)
          \qquad(x\in H).
\tag{2.6}
\]
Because two distinct points of $H$ give different right-hand sides,
bijectivity forces $f(o)=1$, and therefore $f=f_{0}$ from (1.1).

Undoing the preliminary normalisations (sign choices in $(\ast)$,
permuting the five coordinates, and possibly applying
$x\mapsto N+1-f(x)$) produces the complete family of extremal
coordinate-monotone numberings:
\[
\boxed{\;
\mathcal E^{\operatorname{mon}}
=\Bigl\{
\pm\bigl(f_{0}\circ\pi\bigr)\circ
      (\text{independent reflections})
\;:\;\pi\in S_{5}\Bigr\}},
\]
which has cardinality
\[
|\mathcal E^{\operatorname{mon}}|
  =2\cdot5!\cdot2^{5}=7\,680.
\]

That there are no others follows from the steps above:  
every extremal coordinate-monotone $f$ is reduced to
$f_{0}$ by the sign and symmetry operations already listed.

\bigskip
\textbf{Answer to Part (2).}  
\emph{Exactly} the $7\,680$ maps contained in
$\mathcal E^{\operatorname{mon}}$ are coordinate-monotone and
satisfy $\|f\|_{\operatorname{adj}}=g_{\max}$.

----------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: the board is 4-dimensional (7×7×7×7), not 2-dimensional.  
• Many more variables: each cell is indexed by four coordinates, creating 2401 vertices and substantially more adjacencies.  
• Path-length argument in 4-D: establishing the lower bound required understanding the Chebyshev metric in ℤ⁴ and constructing a 6-step diagonal path.  
• Positional-numeral construction: bounding the gap from above uses a mixed-radix (base-7) expansion in four variables, giving the bound 7³+7²+7+1.  Extending the 2-D lexicographic trick to 4-D and proving it works in every adjacency direction is decidedly less obvious.  
• Larger numbers & calculations: the critical quantities (2401 labels, gap 400) are an order of magnitude bigger, making ad-hoc “look-and-guess’’ impossible.  
These additions force the solver to employ multidimensional geometry, combinatorial path arguments, and careful positional-number reasoning—techniques well beyond those needed for the original 8×8 or 11×11 chessboard problems—rendering the variant significantly harder.
